title: Sample Size Effect on Reliability
creator: Dan McCreary
subject: Ecology
description: Interactive MicroSim demonstrating how sample size affects the reliability of ecological sampling estimates through repeated sampling visualization.

learningObjectives: Compare the reliability of conclusions drawn from small versus large sample sizes
Explain why larger samples produce more reliable estimates of population parameters
Evaluate ecological research claims by considering sample size and variability
Recognize how misleading conclusions can arise from small or cherry-picked samples
prerequisites: Basic understanding of averages and percentages
Understanding that scientists study samples because they cannot measure entire populations

pedagogical.teachingStrategy: Repeated sampling simulation with statistical visualization
keyQuestions: Why do small samples produce more variable estimates than large samples?
How confident should you be in a study based on only 8 animals?
What is a minimum sample size for reliable ecological estimates?
How does understanding sample size help evaluate environmental news claims?
Why might two studies of the same species report very different survival rates?
commonMisconceptions: A single sample of any size gives the true population value
Doubling the sample size doubles the accuracy of the estimate
Small samples are unreliable because of bias rather than random variation
assessmentOpportunities: Students compare variability statistics across sample sizes in a data table
Students evaluate fake headlines generated from extreme small samples
Students propose minimum sample sizes for hypothetical ecological studies
